key_insight: Intrinsic ductility in metals can be predicted by a lower-energy shear-amorphization criterion instead of traditional crack-cleavage or dislocation-based fracture models.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical design rule for screening pure metals and multi-principal-element alloys for strength and room-temperature ductility, especially in structural alloy development.
method: Derive structure-property relations from valence charge distribution, band filling, and shear strain effects.
Use analytical expressions with tabulated ab-initio stiffness constants, lattice parameters, and binary interaction energies.
Generate phase diagrams for the Nb-Ta-V-Ti alloy system to map ductility and strength trends.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that the model reproduces intrinsic ductility and ductile-to-brittle transition temperatures and is validated against phase-diagram behavior in Nb-Ta-V-Ti.
watch_item: The framework depends on available ab-initio constants and interaction data, so predictive accuracy may be limited for systems with sparse or uncertain materials inputs.
paper_title: Intrinsic Ductility from Shear Amorphization: From Pure Metals to Multi-Principal-Element Alloys
